coredata.dc:title: Accelerated naked-eye identification of cations using non-plasmonic amplification by functionalized GQDs: A novel photochemical microfluidic sensor for environmental/mineral analysis using Lab-on-paper technology
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Abdollahian, Parinaz
Heidari, Hassan
Hassanzadeh, Soheila
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: This study describes the synthesis of various types of thiolated-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the simultaneous multi-sensing of heavy metal ions using one-droplet microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (OD-µPADs). For the first time, functionalized GQDs were synthesized and stabilized on the surface of hydrophobic fiber-glass with simple manufacturing and assembly strategy towards colorimetric chemosensing of ions including iron, copper, cobalt, mercury, chrome, manganese, and nickel. For this purpose, GQDs are functionalized by various organic species including cysteamine, decanethiol, and mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) towards specific electrostatic interaction with metal ions of Fe (II), Cu (II), Co(II), Hg (II), Mn (II), and Ni (II). Therefore, an innovative GQDs-based miniaturized chemo-system was proposed for the environmental analysis. Additionally, the results were confirmed using fluorescence spectrometry as an optical detection method. Finally, this simple and efficient colorimetric platform was used for facile metal ions detection in human urine models, and environmental fluids, which can provide a commercial device for sensing contaminants by non-professional users in biomedical/environmental mixture. As an economical technique, this platform presents a “mix-and-sense” system without dye (as a side substance) or chemical treatments. The low instrumental requirements, the color uniformity, and the disposability of OD-μPADs approve the commercial application of the engineered ion-chemosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: One-droplet microfluid
Optical chemosensor
Advanced nanomaterials
Graphene quantum dots
Heavy metal
Environmental protection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of thiol-functionalized graphene quantum dots (TGQDs)
Colorimetric detection of heavy metal ions by TGQDs sensing probes
Colorimetric detection of different concentrations of the heavy metal ions by TGQDs sensing probs
Fluorometric detection of heavy metal ions by TGQDs sensing probs in biological samples
Selective detection of heavy metal ions by TGQD, using μPADs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Tabriz, Iran) to support of this study (Grant No. 72824).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Colorimetric detection of the copper, cobalt, mercury, iron, chromium, and manganese ions in standard solution, with 1 µg/ml concentration, by GQD-Cys A sensing prob (100 μl/200 μl/100 μl for TGQDs/dilution buffer/heavy metal ion).
Different concentrations of different ions in urine and water samples complex with the thiolated GQD solutions.
The fluorimetry intensity of the GQD-DDT prob synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid, in the presence of different ions in urine sample. The concentration of all ions are 1000 ppm.
The fluorimetry intensity of the GQD-DDT prob synthesized by hydrolysis in the presence of different ions in urine sample. The concentration of all ions are 1000 ppm.
Emission peak of the GQD-MPA prob synthesized by hydrolysis in the presence of the thiophilic ions in urine sample. The concentration of all ions are 1000 ppm.
Emission peak of the hydrothermally synthesized GQD-MPA prob in the presence of the thiophilic ions. The concentration of all ions are 1000 ppm.
Colorimetric sensing of heavy metal ionsby TGQDs using μPADs platform.
The interaction mechanism of TGQD with thiophilic heavy metal ions.

head.ce:title: Accelerated naked-eye identification of cations using non-plasmonic amplification by functionalized GQDs: A novel photochemical microfluidic sensor for environmental/mineral analysis using Lab-on-paper technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nutrition Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nutrition Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Mining, Petroleum and Geophysics, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study describes the synthesis of various types of thiolated-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the simultaneous multi-sensing of heavy metal ions using one-droplet microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (OD-µPADs). For the first time, functionalized GQDs were synthesized and stabilized on the surface of hydrophobic fiber-glass with simple manufacturing and assembly strategy towards colorimetric chemosensing of ions including iron, copper, cobalt, mercury, chrome, manganese, and nickel. For this purpose, GQDs are functionalized by various organic species including cysteamine, decanethiol, and mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) towards specific electrostatic interaction with metal ions of Fe (II), Cu (II), Co(II), Hg (II), Mn (II), and Ni (II). Therefore, an innovative GQDs-based miniaturized chemo-system was proposed for the environmental analysis. Additionally, the results were confirmed using fluorescence spectrometry as an optical detection method. Finally, this simple and efficient colorimetric platform was used for facile metal ions detection in human urine models, and environmental fluids, which can provide a commercial device for sensing contaminants by non-professional users in biomedical/environmental mixture. As an economical technique, this platform presents a “mix-and-sense” system without dye (as a side substance) or chemical treatments. The low instrumental requirements, the color uniformity, and the disposability of OD-μPADs approve the commercial application of the engineered ion-chemosensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the industrial revolution, human exposure to hazardous heavy metal ions has dramatically increased. Industrial productions are the main causes of ion discharging into nature (soil, water, etc.) which further can contaminate the food chain and human body [1]. Among the most prevalent heavy metal ions in water and food, mercury (Hg2+), chromium (Cr2+) [2], Cobalt (Co2+) [3], and manganese (Mn2+) [4] cause human poisoning. Excessive exposure of the heavy metal ions in humans’ bodies results to several dysfunctions including cell apoptosis, ROS generation, and enzyme inactivation in different organs and tissues [5]. For example, chromium and mercury are chief causes of genomic instability, carcinogenicity, kidney disorders, and bloody diarrhea [6]. On the contrary, low dosages of some vital ions in the body such as iron (Fe2+) [7], manganese (Mn2+) [8], and copper (Cu2+) [9] cause neural disabilities including anxiety, Parkinson’s disease, and fatigue.

Identifying heavy metal ions in environmental and biological fluids is a challenging area in human health studies. Despite significant progress in heavy metal ions recognition, poisoning remains a significant issue that needs effective and preventive treatment [10]. To examine heavy metal ions, analytical techniques have been used, including atomic absorption spectrometry, ion imprinting technology, electrochemistry, and fluorescence methods [11]. However, these sensing methods are confronting some problems, such as complex operation, precise and expensive instruments, and high professional conditions [12]. In contrast,colorimetric sensing of metal ions has garnered significant attention owing to their prominent advantages, particularly in on-site and rapid recognition. Chief among their benefits, colorimetric sensing can detect heavy metal ions by the naked eye without developed equipment, which makes this method useful for non-professionals [13].

Colorimetric sensing is a widely used method for detecting heavy metal ions in a solution. It entails the utilization of specific chemical reagents that can bind to the heavy metal ions, resulting in a color change that can be easily detected by the naked eye or measured using a spectrophotometer [14]. One common approach for colorimetric sensing of heavy metal ions is based on the use of chelating agents, which are chemical compounds that have a high affinity for metal ions. These agents can selectively bind to the metal ions of interest, forming stable complexes that exhibit a distinct color change. For example, the use of dithizone as a chelating agent can lead to the formation of a red complex with mercury ions [15]. Another approach is to use nanoparticles as colorimetric sensors. Nanoparticles can be synthesized with specific properties that enable them to bind to heavy metal ions and induce a color change. For example, gold nanoparticles can be functionalized with thiol groups that can selectively bind to mercury ions, resulting in a change in the color of the solution from red to blue [16].

Previously, gold nanoparticles (AuNP) were synthesized by citrate-based reduction technique and functionalized with rhodamine and hexane dithiol which could detect Hg2+ with detection limit (LOD) of 1 ng/mL−1. In another study, researchers used dithioerythritol as stabilizing and capping agent of AuNPs to sense Hg2+ with LOD of 24 nM. Moreover, trithiocyanuric acid treated AuNPs, and aminopropyltriethoxysilane functionalized AuNPs were used for Hg2+ detection with LOD of 2.8 nM, and 10 nM, respectively. In our previous works, we used microfluidic paper-based platforms for in situ sensing of heavy metal ions. We used cysteamine-capped gold nanoparticles which could sense Hg2+ with LLOQ of 0.001 ppm in the presence of cysteine, and Cu2+ with LLOQ of 0.02 ppm in the presence of methionine. The sensing mechanism was based on the agglomeration of aminated AuNPs by amino acids, and their deagglomeration in the presence of heavy metal ions, attributed to the chelating behavior of cysteine and methionine towards Hg2+, and Cu2+, respectively [17]. In another study, we used GQDs capped D-penicillamine (DPA) for the colorimetric and fluorometric detection of Cu2+ with LLOQ of 0.1 ppm, respecting to the chelating behavior of DPA towards the copper ion [18]. Also, by incomplete hydrothermal pyrolysisof citric acid, we found the graphene-based sensing prob could further sense Fe3+, and vanadium ion (V4+) with LLOQ of 0.01 ppm in linear range of 0.01 to 1 ppm [19].

Fluorimetry sensing is another method that can be used for the detection of heavy metal ions. Fluorimetry sensing relies on the measurement of fluorescence emission from a fluorescent probe in the presence of these ions [20]. One common approach involves using chelating fluorescent probes that can selectively bind to the heavy metal ions of interest. The binding of the probe to the metal ion can result in a change in the fluorescence intensity or wavelength, which can be detected and measured [21]. For example, the use of 8-hydroxyquinoline as a chelating probe can result in a fluorescence enhancement in the presence of aluminum ions. Another approach is to use QDs as fluorescent probes [22].

QDs are semiconductor nanoparticles capable of emitting fluorescence at specific wavelengths when excited with light. QDs can be functionalized with specific ligands that selectively bind to heavy metal ions, resulting in a shift in the emission wavelength of the QD. This shift can be measured to determine the presence and concentration of the heavy metal ion of interest [23].

GQDs are a promising material for the detection of heavy metal ions due to their unique optical and electronic properties. GQDs are small graphene fragments with sizes less than 10 nm and exhibit tunable optical and electronic properties, making them attractive for sensing applications [24]. One approach to using GQDs as heavy metal ions sensors involve functionalizing the GQDs with specific functional groups that can selectively bind to heavy metal ions. For example, carboxyl or amino functional groups can be added to the surface of GQDs, which can bind to heavy metal ions such as lead or mercury ions [25]. The binding of heavy metal ions to the GQDs can result in changes in the optical properties of the GQDs, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancement. These changes can be detected and measured using fluorescence spectroscopy, providing a sensitive and selective method for detecting heavy metal ions [26]. In addition to their sensing capabilities, GQDs also have several other advantages, such as high stability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, making them attractive for various applications, including environmental monitoring, food safety, and biomedical sensing [27].

In this study, we introduced a multifunctional platform for sensing heavy metal ions with a sensitive, fast response, and high stable colorimetric response towards iron, chromium, manganese, copper, and cobalt heavy metal ions. All the mentioned metal ions can be detected with distinct colors on designed multifunctional microfluidic paper-based platform (µPAD). The provided OD-µPAD is a sensing device with commercial applications for farmers, miners, aquatic studies, etc., taking advantage of its robust, user-friendly visual sensing, besides its low-cost, and facile synthesis. To the best of our knowledge, there is not any similar platforms for simultaneous multi-sensing of ions by OD-µPAD, which is useful for both environmental and biomedical applications. Here, thiolated GQDs were applied as sensing prob of metal ions. The sensing probs were fabricated by functionalizing GQD with cysteamine, do-decanethiol (DDT), and mercaptopropionic acid (MPA). Colorimetric changes were obtained both in the liquid form, and on the OD-µPAD. The colorimetric results were then confirmed by fluorimetry.

Sodium hydroxide(NaOH),citric acid, D-penicillamine (DPA), and acetone were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The standard solutions of (Fe(III), Hg(II), Zr(IV), Mn(II), Ba(II), Sr(II), Mg(II), B(III), Al(III), As(III), K(I), V(III), Mo(II), Na(I), Zn(II), Co(II), Se(IV), Sn(II), Bi(III), Cr(III)) (with 1 μg/ml concentration) were acquired from chemlab (Zedelgem, Belgium), with high grade of analytical purity. All solutions were prepared bydeionized water. Paraffin gained from local shop.

The morphology and size of the GQD-DPA nanoparticles were examined bytransmission electron microscopy(TEM) via a Hitachi H-800 TEM setup (200 kV).’ Was deleted from section 2.2. The absorption spectra were recorded by UV–Visspectroscopy, via U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded through a fluorescence spectrophotometer setup (Cary Eclipse (Agilent, Japan)).A Nicolet FT-IR 6700spectrometers was used for Infrared spectrarecording. Atomic force microscopic (AFM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were achieved by a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). And Siemens, D500 (Germany), respectively. DLS analysis was done by PerkinElmer (Aachen, Germany) setup. Size of the particles were measured by Malvern particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of thiol-functionalized graphene quantum dots (TGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: We used two methods for TGQD synthesis. GQD was synthesized by hydrolysis, and by hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid, separately. For the synthesis of GQD, 2 g of citric acid was heated on a hot plate in 200 °C for 30 min. Then 100 ml sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution (10 mg/ml) was added. Afterwards, acetone was added to the solution, causing it to divide the solution in two separate phases. The precipitated phase is GQD which was extracted and kept at 4 °C. Then, 2 g of the lyophilized GQD solution was mixed with the solution of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and deionized water with same proportion (1:1). Also, 0. 8 g of dodecanethiol (DDT), 0.304 gr of cysteamine, and 0.318 g of MPA were added to the mixture separately, followed by stirring for 24 h in 60 °C. Afterwards, 0.5 g of N, N'-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and 0.5 g of 4-Dimethylaminopyridine were added to the solution and stirred for another 24 h. The solution was dried, and the remaining powder was collected. The GQD-DDT powder was mixed with 0.1 M sodium hydroxide at a concentration of 1 mg/ml and homogenized using an ultrasonic bath. Therefore, DDT-GQD, Cysteamine-GQD, and MPA-GQD were fabricated.

In the other method, TGQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal interaction of citric acid with the thiol-containing materials (DDT, MPA, and CysA). In this approach, the TGQD solutions were prepared by incubation of the citric acid and DDT, MPA, and CysA solutions in a Teflon flask which was incubated in 200 °C for 2 h. Therefore, GQD-CysA, GQD-DDT, and GQD-MPA were prepared.

With the aim of colorimetric sensing of ions, the solutions of heavy metal ions (Fe(III), Hg(II), Zr(IV), Mn(II), Mg(II),Ba(II), As(III), Bi(III)), Sr(II), Al(III), B(III), K(I),V(III), Mo(II), Na(I), Co(II), Sn(II), Zn(II), Cr(III), and Se(IV) (1 μg/ml, 200 μl) were mixed with the solutions of TGQDs (1:1).

Moreover, various concentrations of iron, copper, cobalt, mercury, chrome, manganese, and nickel heavy metal ions (0.001 ppm to 5 ppm) were assessed via colorimetric detection, using TGQDs as the sensing probs. A Samsung note (II) system camera (600-dpi resolution) was used for the optical images.

To miniaturize of the proposed colorimetric chemosensor for the recognition of candidate ions, the fabrication of optimized µPCD was done. For this, a fiberglass circular paper was impregnated with paraffin in determined regions of the paper for creation of eight channel connected to a well in the center. The fabrication procedure is straightforward. To begin, paraffin was heated until it became a liquid, and using a hot plate, the metal stencil was heated to 165° C (optimized temperature). The fiberglass paper was then dipped in liquid paraffin and dried. The Paraffin-impregnated microfluidic paper was then placed in contact with fresh microfluidic paper between the stencil and the magnet for 35 s (optimized time) so that the fresh paper took the pre-designed hydrophobic and semi- hydrophobic areas of paraffin due to the temperature and pressure.

The fluorescent sensing of the ions was performed after adding the ions to TGQD. The solutions of heavy metal ions (1 ppm) were mixed with the solutions of TGQD (1 M) with (1:1) ratio. The fluorimetry spectra of the mixtures were revealed by fluorescence spectrophotometer (n = 3, central wavelength of 346 nm).

With the aim of optical sensing, graphene-derivatives are efficient materials owing to their fluorescence properties. Graphene's exceptional properties, such as high electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, and large surface area, make it an ideal choice for sensitive sensors. Here, we usedcitric acidto produce GQD. To increase the fluorometry intensity, the electron-rich thiol groups were conjugated to GQD, using cysteamine, MPA, and DDT. Some specific heavy metal ions (iron, copper, cobalt, mercury, chrome, manganese, and nickel) caused the TGQD agglomeration, due to the interactions with the thiol groups. The color alternation of the sensing prob was depended on the rate of aggregation of GQDs, which also affects its fluorimetry peak intensity.

GQDs were synthesized and characterized according to our previous report [19]. Size measurement by DLS and Zeta potential shows the GQD's final size is around 0.8 nm (Fig. S1A&B). The XRD pattern in Fig. S1C indicates a broad peak at approximately 25θ (0.36 nm) for GQDs, suggesting more exposed functional sites compared to graphene. The higher peak intensity in GQD, when compared to graphene, is likely due to citric acid carbonization, resulting in graphite structures with denser interlayer spacing. Fig. S1D in the supporting information displays the TEM image of GQDs, revealing the uniform distribution of GQD particles. The high temperatures used in the process promote the formation of GQDs with relatively uniform size. Moreover, the distribution characteristics of the graphene sheets can be controlled in the process of creating a zero-band-gap semiconductor, resulting in the production of a finely dispersed GQDs solution.

The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs exhibits a peak at 3434 cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric stretching vibration of amine groups (–NH2). Peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 1691 cm−1 can be attributed to C–H and CO stretching vibrations, while peaks at 1766 and 1716 cm−1 relate to CO stretching vibrations in GQDs' FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2E). All of these results were confirmed by Uv–Vis spectra (Fig. S1F). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQD (Fig. 2A) illustrate typical absorption of graphene derivatives in the UV region (200–300 nm) with an intensive peak at 340 nm and also a peak at 210 nm.

High electron load weakens C-O bonds and reduces stability in GQD sheets. Functionalization can also lower fluorescence intensity. To address this, a new method involves thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and thiophilic materials for TGQD synthesis. Varying citric acid amounts affect sheet size, while adjusting thiophilic materials alters particle size due to chemical reactions. An excessive citric acid-to-functionalizing material ratio inhibits GQD formation. The best results occur at a mass ratio of functionalizing group to citric acid around 0.4, as in our experiment.

Moreover, TGQDs were synthesized by the thermal hydrolysis of citric acid with DDT, Cys-A, and MPA, as thiol-included chemicals. The hydrothermal synthesis method successfully produced graphene quantum dots functionalized with DDT, Cys-A, and MPA. UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy measurements demonstrated tunable luminescent properties, indicating controlled bandgap engineering through the functionalization process. The hydrothermal synthesis method described in this study enables the fabrication of graphene quantum dots functionalized with dodecanethiol. The resulting quantum dots exhibit tunable luminescent properties, making them promising candidates for various luminescence-based applications. This approach provides a pathway for controlling the band-gap of graphene and expanding its direct application in luminescence studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of heavy metal ions by TGQDs sensing probes
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of copper, cobalt, mercury, iron, chromium, and manganese, the color of the TGQDs sensing prob was changed, though, other ions didn’t show significant effects on the color of the sensing solution (Fig. 2 and Figs S2-S6 (see Supporting information)). According to the chemical structures of the sensing probes (DDT, cysteamine, and MPA), TGQDs were fabricated by binding between the amine group of cysteamine and the carboxylic acid group of GQDs, and binding between carboxylic acid group of MPA and the amine groups of GQD. For TGQD production via DDT, the carboxylic acid groups of GQDs are binding with the thiol groups of DDT in one-step TGQDs fabrication via pyrolysis, and the hydroxyl group of GQD with the CH3 group of DDT in two-step fabrication of TGQD by DDT. As mentioned before, in exposure to the thiophilic materials, free carboxyl groups of GQDs make interactions with the thiol functional groups, leading to fabricate TGQDs with free carboxyl and amine groups (Scheme 1 ).

Therefore, heavy metal ions such as copper, cobalt, mercury, iron, chromium, and manganese have a tendency to interact with the thiol groups with different rates which results to various colors illustrated in Fig. 1 , and Figs. S4–S5 (see Supporting information).

The final TGQDs obtained through hydrothermal functionalization of GQD exhibit differences in several aspects. These include size distribution, luminescent properties, surface functionalization, and structural characteristics. GQDs produced via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid tend to have a narrow size distribution. Regarding luminescent properties, GQDs synthesized through pyrolysis of citric acid often exhibit enhanced fluorescence emission. The high-temperature conditions facilitate the formation of GQDs with efficient luminescence, making them suitable for luminescence-based applications. On the other hand, GQDs obtained through pyrolysis may have different luminescent properties, as the lower temperatures used in this method can result in varied luminescence efficiency. Surface functionalization also differs between the two methods. GQDs synthesized via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid typically have a lower degree of surface functionalization. The high temperatures during the process limit the attachment of functional groups on the GQD surface. In contrast, GQDs obtained from hydrolysis offer more opportunities for surface functionalization. The hydrolysis process introduces functional groups onto the GQD surface, allowing for further modification and tailored applications. Therefore, Figure S4 and S5 (see supporting information) shows the detection of 7 different ions, including Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cr2+, and Ni2+ with distinct colors by hydrolysis synthesis of GQD-CysA, GQD-DDT, and GQD-MPA. However, the TGQDs prepared by hydrothermal synthesis method could detect only 4 different ions by GQD-MPA (including V3+, Pt4+, Cu2+, and Hg2+) (Fig. S8 (see Supporting information)), one ion by GQD-Cys-A (Hg2+) (Fig. S8 (see Supporting information)), and two ions by GQD-DDT including Bi3+, and V5+ (Fig. S9 (see Supporting information)). The structural characteristics of the GQDs also vary. GQDs synthesized through thermal pyrolysis of citric acid typically possess a crystalline structure with well-defined edges and defects. The high-temperature of the hydrothermal process promotes the formation of graphene-like structures within the GQDs. Conversely, GQDs obtained from hydrolysis may have a more amorphous or disordered structure. The lower temperatures and hydrolytic conditions result in GQDs with a less-defined crystalline structure. It is important to note that the specific properties of GQDs can vary based on the synthesis parameters, reaction conditions, and characterization techniques employed. Both hydrolysis synthesis and thermal pyrolysis offer different approaches for obtaining GQDs with distinct characteristics, allowing for a range of applications in various fields. For example, hydrothermal synthesis of GQD-DDT, which can sense Bi3+ can be used for can help assess the quality and safety of drinking water and identify sources of pollution. Also, bismuth ions can be used as catalysts or catalyst precursors in various chemical reactions and industrial processes. Monitoring and controlling the concentration of bismuth ions in these processes is crucial for optimizing reaction efficiency and product quality. In other hand, thermal pyrolysis-synthesized GQD-CysA can detect mercury ion.

The videos depicting the color changes of the TGQD are included in the appendix file. Videos 1 to 3 are related to the interactions between the heavy metal ions and hydrolysis-synthesized GQD-CysA, pyrolysis-synthesized GQD-DDT, and hydrolysis-synthesized GQD-MPA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of different concentrations of the heavy metal ions by TGQDs sensing probs
ce:section.ce:para: The extent of GQD agglomeration due to heavy metal ions is linked to solution color. Different ion concentrations cause varying GQD accumulations. Fig. 2 demonstrates colorimetric sensing of these ions by TGQD optical probes in both water and biological samples. The interaction with heavy metal ions can change GQD aggregation and optical properties, affecting solution color. When GQDs are well-dispersed, their size and surface chemistry define a characteristic color. Heavy metal ions can induce agglomeration, altering the solution color. Agglomeration degree depends on ion concentration, GQD surface chemistry, and the specific heavy metal. Thiophilic metal ions promote GQD aggregation, influencing solution color. Agglomeration affects color intensity, wavelength, and appearance. The relationship between agglomeration and color isn't linear due to ion-GQD interactions, pH, temperature, and other factors. Solution color visually indicates GQD agglomeration by heavy metal ions. Monitoring color changes provides insights into their interactions and concentrations. Thiophilic metal ion concentration influences agglomeration and the resulting color change. Higher concentrations lead to more noticeable color changes, allowing estimation of metal ion concentration. This colorimetric sensing approach offers a simple and visually accessible method for detecting of these metal ions in various samples, including environmental samples, biological fluids, or industrial wastewater (Fig. 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of heavy metal ions by TGQDs sensing probs in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding the detection process, Thiophilic metal ions have a strong affinity for thiol groups, which can form ring-like structures known as chelates around the metal ions. Chelation occurs when one or two covalent bonds are formed between the metal ion and the ligand atoms. Ligand atoms that commonly participate in chelation include nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and oxygen (O), which are present in chemical groups such as S-S, NH2, OH, NH, and SH. Thiophilic metal ions have a high tendency to bind to thiol groups due to their soft Lewis acid nature. Thiol groups contain a sulfur atom with a lone pair of electrons that can coordinate with the metal ion, forming a stable chelate complex. Multidentate ligands generate ring constructions which includes the metal moiety and the two-ligand molecules linked to the metal. Several donors act as bi-dentate ligands. In the elementary model of chelation, a proton (H+) attracts to an electron of the chelator material by electrostatic binds which might be involved in the chelation process. The chelation behavior of the thiophilic metal ions is related to the level of thiophilicity of the metal ions. Indeed, thiophilic materials, including mercury (Hg), copper (Cu), and cobalt (Co), exhibit varying rates of interaction with thiol groups based on their level of thiophilicity. Fig. 3 , and Fig. S12 (see supporting information) show the emission peak intensities of different heavy metal ions in the hydrothermally synthesized GQD-DDT via the thiophilic interactions, in urine and water samples, respectively. Thiophilicity refers to the affinity or preference of a material for thiol (sulfur-containing) groups. Among these materials, mercury is known to possess the highest thiophilicity. It has a strong affinity for thiol groups and forms stable complexes with sulfur atoms. Mercury readily forms covalent bonds with thiol groups, leading to the formation of mercury-thiol complexes. Copper also exhibits a considerable level of thiophilicity. It can interact with thiol groups, although its affinity may be lower compared to mercury. Copper ions can coordinate with sulfur atoms in thiol groups, forming copper-thiol complexes. Cobalt, while demonstrating some degree of thiophilicity, may have a lower affinity for thiol groups compared to mercury and copper. Cobalt ions can interact with thiol groups, but the interaction may be less strong or less favored compared to the other two materials. Iron (Fe) ions also exhibit thiophilicity, albeit to a lesser extent compared to mercury, copper, and cobalt. Iron is a transition metal and can interact with thiol groups, forming iron-thiol complexes. Iron ions have a moderate affinity for thiol groups, and their interaction with sulfur atoms in thiols can result in the formation of stable complexes. However, the thiophilicity of iron is generally lower than that of mercury and copper. Iron-thiol interactions are influenced by factors such as the oxidation state of iron, the ligand environment, and the presence of other competing ligands. Bismuth (Bi) is known for its moderate thiophilicity, which refers to its affinity or preference for thiol (sulfur-containing) groups. While not as strong as some other metals like mercury or copper, bismuth still exhibits a tendency to interact with thiols and form stable complexes. The thiophilicity of bismuth arises from its electronic configuration and atomic properties. Bismuth has relatively low electronegativity and a large atomic radius, which allows for a more polarizable electron cloud. This property enables bismuth to interact with the electron-rich sulfur atoms in thiol groups.

The variation in thiophilicity among different heavy metal ions is attributed to differences in their electronic configurations, atomic sizes, and electronegativities. These factors influence the strength of the metal-thiol bond and the stability of the resulting complexes. The emission spectrum of GQD-DDT in the presence of different heavy metal ions is illustrated in Fig. 3. According to this figure, the intensity of the fluorimetry peak of GQD decreased after functionalizing with DDT. The reason is attributed to the interactions between the thiol groups via S-S bonds which gathered GQDs and caused the decrease in the intensity of the fluorimetry peak. When mercury was introduced to the system the thiol groups detached TGQDs to accumulate Hg2+ ions with high thiophilicity. This deaccumulation of the GQDs causes the fluorimetry peak intensity to increase of TGQD in the presence of Hg2+. In comparison to Hg2+, the intensity of the fluorimetry peak of TGQD was decreased by adding Cu2+, which is related to the lowest tendency of Cu ion to interact with the Thiol functional groups. Therefore, GQDs with lower thiophilic groups had reduced interaction with copper ions compared to mercury ions, resulting in lesser deagglomeration of GQDs in the presence of copper ions. Similar results were observed with TGQD in the presence of cobalt and iron ions, as their lower thiophilicity compared to mercury and copper ions led to reduced deagglomeration of TGQDs.

However, Fig. 3 demonstrates that the fluorimetry peak intensity of TGQD was diminished, compared to its bare state, in the presence of Bi3+ and Cr2+ ions. The observed reduction in the fluorimetry peak intensity of TGQD in the presence of Bi3+ and Cr2+ ions can be attributed to the tendency of these ions to interact with GQDs instead of the thiol groups. This behavior is likely due to their low thiophilicity, which results in a preference for interacting with the GQD surface rather than forming strong coordination bonds with thiol groups. The interaction between bismuth and GQDs can occur through several mechanisms. Bismuth ions have a moderate thiophilicity and can interact with the surface functional groups of GQDs. One possible interaction is the coordination of bismuth ions with thiol groups present on the GQD surface. Bismuth ions have an affinity for thiol groups and can form coordination bonds with the sulfur atoms, resulting in the attachment of bismuth species onto the GQD surface. Another interaction mechanism involves the adsorption of bismuth species onto the surface of GQDs. Bismuth-containing compounds or bismuth nanoparticles can adsorb onto the GQD surface through attractive forces such as electrostatic interactions or Van der Waals forces. The interaction between bismuth and GQDs can also involve chemical reactions that modify the surface functional groups. This causes a shift in the emission peak of TGQD after interaction with Bi3+ ions.

Fig. 4 , and Fig. S10 (see Supporting information) show the alternations in the emission peak of the hydrolysis synthesis GQD-DDT by different heavy metal ions in urine sample, and water sample, respectively. The intensity of the emission peak of GQD-DDT, synthesized by the hydrolysis method, can increase in the presence of copper ions (Cu2+), mercury ions (Hg2+), and manganese ions (Mn2+). When GQDs functionalized with dodecanethiol are exposed to copper ions, the coordination and complexation between the thiol groups of dodecanethiol and copper ions can occur. This interaction can lead to the enhancement of the emission intensity of the GQDs, resulting in a higher fluorescence signal. Similarly, the presence of mercury ions can also lead to an increase in the emission intensity of GQDs functionalized with dodecanethiol. The thiol groups of dodecanethiol can coordinate with the mercury ions, resulting in a complex formation. This interaction can alter the energy transfer pathways within the GQDs, leading to an enhancement of the emission intensity. In the case of manganese ions, their interaction with GQDs functionalized with dodecanethiol can also lead to an increase in the emission intensity. The exact mechanism of this enhancement may involve coordination and complex formation between the thiol groups and manganese ions, leading to modifications in the electronic and optical properties of the GQDs (Fig. 4).

Fig. 5 shows the emission peaks of GQD-MPA in the presence of heavy metal ions in the urine sample of the patients. The presence of iron caused the increment in the TGQD emission peak. In the presence of iron ions, the carboxylate group of MPA can form coordination bonds with the iron ions, leading to the formation of complexes. The thiol group of MPA can also interact with iron ions through coordination, forming coordination bonds between the iron and sulfur atoms. The presence of GQDs can facilitate the interaction between MPA and iron ions. The functional groups on the GQD surface, such as oxygen-containing groups and π-electron systems, can provide additional binding sites for iron ions, enhancing the coordination and complexation process. Therefore, deagglomeration might occur in GQDs, leading to an increase in the intensity of the emission peak in the presence of iron ions. In a similar manner, the interaction between GQDs functionalized with MPA and chromium ions involves coordination and complex formation. When chromium ions are present, the carboxylate group of MPA can form coordination bonds with the chromium ions, resulting in the formation of complexes. The thiol group of MPA can also interact with chromium ions through coordination, forming coordination bonds between the chromium and sulfur atoms, leading to an increase in the intensity of the fluorescence peak. On the other hand, mercury and copper ions have a higher affinity for thiol groups (-SH) compared to that of iron ions. In the presence of mercury ions, the thiol groups on MPA may preferentially interact and coordinate with the mercury ions, forming stable complexes. This interaction between mercury ions and thiol groups can lead to the quenching or reduction of the fluorescence emitted by the GQDs (Fig. 5).

Fig. 6 , and Fig. S7 (see supporting information) show the alterations in the emission peak of the hydrothermally synthesis GQD-MPA, after interaction with thiophilic heavy metal ions in urine sample, and environmental sample, respectively. In the presence of thiophilic ions, such as mercury, copper, and zinc, the interaction with the thiol groups of MPA on the surface of GQDs can result in a decrease in the emission intensity. Thiophilic ions have a high affinity for sulfur-containing groups, and they can bind to the thiol groups of MPA through coordination and complexation. This interaction can modify the electronic structure and energy transfer processes within the GQDs, leading to a decrease in the emission intensity. The formation of complexes between thiophilic ions and thiol groups can potentially create non-radiative energy pathways or quench the fluorescence of the GQDs. As a result, the emission intensity of the GQDs functionalized with MPA is reduced in the presence of thiophilic ions (Fig. 6).

When cobalt and copper ions are introduced to a solution containing GQDs functionalized with cysteamine, synthesized through hydrolysis method, an interesting observation is made – the peak intensity of the GQD-CysA fluorescence is increased (Fig. S11 (see Supporting information)). This can be attributed to the interaction between the GQDs and the metal ions, which affects the photophysical properties of the GQDs. One possible explanation for this increase in peak intensity is the formation of coordination complexes between the GQDs and the metal ions. Cobalt and copper ions have a tendency to coordinate with ligands containing amine groups, and thiol groups such as cysteamine. The coordination of the metal ions with the amine groups on the GQD-cysteamine surface can lead to a modification of the electronic structure and energy transfer pathways within the GQDs. The coordination complex formation can result in a more favorable energy transfer process, where the excitation energy is efficiently transferred to the GQDs, leading to an increase in the emission intensity. This phenomenon is commonly observed in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) systems, where energy transfer from a donor molecule to an acceptor molecule enhances the emission intensity of the acceptor. Additionally, the presence of cobalt and copper ions may also influence the charge transfer processes within the GQDs. These metal ions can act as electron acceptors, facilitating electron transfer from the GQDs to the metal ions. This electron transfer process can contribute to the increase in emission intensity by altering the radiative processes within the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of heavy metal ions by TGQD, using μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric detection of thiophilic heavy metal ions using thiophil-functionalized graphene quantum dots on a paper platform as a paper-based sensor offers a convenient and low-cost approach for rapid metal ion detection. In this sensing platform, TGQDs are specifically functionalized with thiol groups that have an affinity for thiophilic heavy metal ions such as chrome, copper, or bismuth ions. The presence of these metal ions triggers a color change in the TGQD-coated paper, allowing for visual detection of the metal ions without the need for complex instrumentation. The paper-based sensor is typically prepared by depositing or immobilizing the TGQDs onto a paper substrate, forming a pattern coating. The Thiol functional groups on the TGQDs can selectively bind to the target metal ions, inducing aggregation or complex formation. This results in a noticeable change in the color of the TGQD-coated paper, which can be observed with the naked eye. The color change in the paper-based sensor occurs due to alterations in the optical properties of the TGQDs upon interaction with the metal ions. The specific color change observed is dependent on the metal ion being detected. For instance, the presence of chromium ions may cause a color shift towards blue or violet, copper ions could induce a change towards green, manganese ions may alter the color towards brown, cobalt ions could lead to a color change towards green, iron ions may cause a shift towards orange, and bismuth ions might result in a yellowish color.

Fig. 7 shows the optical photo for colorimetric detection of manganese, iron, cobalt, chrome, and copper ions on the microfluidic paper-based platform. The optical pictures of theμPAD were taken in room light, where the color changes of the TGQD were detected within the μ-capillaries. The cause might be owing to the disruption of TGQDs aggregation after interfering with the heavy metal ions. After 5 min of exposure to TGQDs, the color alteration of the sensing prob appeared, which shows distinct colors for each of the ions (Fig. 7). The difference in the colors is related to the different rates of accumulations of TGQDs, in the presence of different thiophilic ions. Mercury is known to have high tendency to interact with the thiol group. Though, Fig. 7 shows higher intensity of the fluorometry peak in exposure to Cu2+ than that for the mercury. The reason might be due to the interactions between copper and TGDQs not only by the thiol group, but also by the methyl group of DDT [14]. The record of the color alternation process of the hydrothermally fabricated GQD-DDT, and GQD-CysA in the presence of the thiophilic heavy metal ions is presented in the Video 4 (see appendix), Video 5) see appendix), respectively. Also, Video 6 (see appendix) recorded the color alternation of the thermal pyrolysis-synthesized GQD-DDT in the presence of Bi3+. The advantages of using TGQDs in a paper-based sensor include simplicity, rapid response, portability, and low cost. It enables on-site metal ion detection, making it suitable for applications in environmental monitoring, food safety, and point-of-care diagnostics. The colorimetric detection of thiophilic heavy metal ions using TGQDs on a paper platform offers a user-friendly and cost-effective approach for metal ion sensing, making it a promising tool for various practical applications.

To the best of our knowledge, the microfluidic paper-based platform for fast simultaneous sensing of different types of heavy metal ions by one prob has not reported before. The sensing setup is completely user friendly, and it can be used by even non-professional users which paved its application as a commercial product. The under study heavy metal ions are the most common elements which are necessary to be under control in both environmental and biological liquids. Different color alternation of the chemosensor in the presence of heavy metal ions can be considered as a signature for the specific ions to be recognized in the visible light by naked eye. The sensing prob is non-toxic, cost-effective, and facile produced product which can be stable on the paper sensing platform for 1 month. The sensing method is easy, fast, sensitive, and cost-effective. Moreover, the low technical requirements of the stamp, the improved color uniformity, and disposability ofthe sensing platform provides the opportunity for the device to be used as a commercial product for sensing applications.

We provide a simple, sensitive, precise, selective, affordable, and user friendly sensing platformfor simultaneous sensing of different heavy metal ions. This study focuses on the binding between the some ions and the thiol groups of the sensing probs. Different rate of deagglomeration of the sensing prob cause to color alternation. Since the rate of thiophilicity is different for various ions, the specific color change was occurred for each ion. We proposed OD-µPADs for some commericial multi-sensing of copper, manganese, iron, chromium, mercury, cobalt, and nickel with different colors in one scene. The sensing prob is cost-effective, and facile produced product which can be stable on the paper sensing platform for about 1 month. The sensing method is fast, facile, non-expensive, and accurate. Moreover, the low technical requirements of the stamp, the improved color uniformity, and disposability ofthe sensing platform provides the opportunity for the device to be used as a commercial product for sensing applications. To the best of our knowledge, the proposed multi-colorimetric method for selective detection of seven ions in aqueous samples by paper-based microfluidic sensor is totally novel and was not reported previously. This setup can be produced in massive amount as a commercial product in markets.

Parinaz Abdollahian: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hassan Heidari: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Soheila Hassanzadeh: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology. Mohammad Hasanzadeh: Supervision, Conseptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Tabriz, Iran) to support of this study (Grant No. 72824).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115299.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
